message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 has a highly variable clinical presentation, ranging from asymptomatic to severe respiratory symptoms and death. Diabetes seems to be one of the main comorbidities contributing to a worse COVID-19 outcome.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In here we analyze the clinical characteristics and outcomes of diabetic COVID-19 patients Kuwait.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In this single-center, retrospective study of 417 consecutive COVID-19 patients, we analyze and compare disease severity, outcome, associated complications, and clinical laboratory findings between diabetic and non-diabetic COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>COVID-19 patients with diabetes had more ICU admission than non-diabetic COVID-19 patients (20.1% vs. 16.8%, p<0.001). Diabetic COVID-19 patients also recorded higher mortality in comparison to non-diabetic COVID-19 patients (16.7% vs. 12.1%, p<0.001). Diabetic COVID-19 patients had significantly higher prevalence of comorbidities, such as hypertension. Laboratory investigations also highlighted notably higher levels of C-reactive protein in diabetic COVID019 patients and lower estimated glomerular filtration rate. They also showed a higher incidence of complications. logistic regression analysis showed that every 1 mmol/L increase in fasting blood glucose in COVID-19 patients is associated with 1.52 (95% CI: 1.34 – 1.72, p<0.001) times the odds of dying from COVID-19.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Diabetes is a major contributor to worsening outcomes in COVID-19 patients. Understanding the pathophysiology underlining these findings could provide insight into better management and improved outcome of such cases.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights of the Study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>A significantly higher proportion of COVID-19 patients with diabetes mellitus required admission to the ICU.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Higher fasting blood glucose was associated with higher risk of COVID-19 associated mortality.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>COVID-19 patients with diabetes mellitus had significantly higher incidence of complications including sepsis, ARDS, cardiac failure and renal failure.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Clinical Characteristics and Outcomes of COVID-19 patients with diabetes mellitus in Kuwait
